---
title: "Lost GRUB menu after adding RAM on a Dell T110 PowerEdge Server"
date: 2011-10-17
forum: Dell Ubuntu Support (CLOSED)
---

### Post by ptmoy on 2011-10-17
I've been dual booting Ubuntu 10.04 desktop (64bit) with Window 7 (64  bit) on a Dell T110 PowerEdge Server (Server has 2GB RAM) since version  10.04 was first released.  Everything has been working fine.  Recently, I  added memory by replacing my 2GB stick with two 4GB sticks for a total  of 8GB.  After this, the GRUB menu for choosing which OS to boot no  longer comes up.  Instead, the screen just flashes a few time, then  computer boots into the default OS, which in my case is Ubuntu 10.04.   If I take out the new RAM and reinstall the original 2GB stick,  everything works as before.  If I keep the 8GB in, and uninstall GRUB-PC  and GRUB COMMON, I get the menu back but still can't boot into Windows  7, but I can still choose to boot into Ubuntu or Memtest without any  problems.  If I re-install GRUB-PC and GRUB COMMON with the 8GB,  original problem comes right back.

Does anyone know what's causing this behavior, and have any suggestion on how I can get GRUB to work with the added memory?

---

### Post by drroller on 2012-06-18
I have this exact problem using Ubuntu 12.04 64 bit server with desktop installed.

I added one 2GB stick to my existing 2GB to take it up to 4GB and now Grub won't load, it just reboots over and over.

Did you ever find an solution?

I know the system is fine because I am booted into the Ubuntu 12.04 livecd now.

EDIT: Update- When I boot into the Ubuntu 64 bit live cd, I can boot, but install crashes when I run it.

Also, I ran memtest86 and it passed with no errors.

---

### Post by blackflame2996 on 2012-06-20
try booting through a supergrub2 live disk.

[http://www.supergrubdisk.org/super-grub2-disk/](http://www.supergrubdisk.org/super-grub2-disk/)

---

### Post by YannBuntu on 2012-06-20
> **ptmoy said:**
> I've been dual booting Ubuntu 10.04 desktop (64bit) with Window 7 (64  bit) on a Dell T110 PowerEdge Server (Server has 2GB RAM) since version  10.04 was first released.  Everything has been working fine.  Recently, I  added memory by replacing my 2GB stick with two 4GB sticks for a total  of 8GB.  After this, the GRUB menu for choosing which OS to boot no  longer comes up.  Instead, the screen just flashes a few time, then  computer boots into the default OS, which in my case is Ubuntu 10.04.   If I take out the new RAM and reinstall the original 2GB stick,  everything works as before.  If I keep the 8GB in, and uninstall GRUB-PC  and GRUB COMMON, I get the menu back but still can't boot into Windows  7, but I can still choose to boot into Ubuntu or Memtest without any  problems.  If I re-install GRUB-PC and GRUB COMMON with the 8GB,  original problem comes right back.

Does anyone know what's causing this behavior, and have any suggestion on how I can get GRUB to work with the added memory?

Can you boot a Ubuntu 10.04 CD when you have 8GB?

---

### Post by ptmoy on 2012-12-24
I never did find a solution to the problem I posted initially, so have been ignoring it.  Since I couldn't get to the Windows 7 boot via grub with the added memory, I've been using the server only with Ubuntu 10.04.  Recently, I added an SSD and tried to install Ubuntu 12.04 on it.  Everything worked except I can't get the system to use any of the proprietary Nvidia drivers no matter what I do (my system has an Nvidia GT240 card in it).  In researching the problem, I accidentally stumbled onto a work around for the Grub-menu-not-displaying problem (see [http://askubuntu.com/questions/161297/why-does-my-screen-blank-out-for-the-duration-of-the-grub-boot-menu](http://askubuntu.com/questions/161297/why-does-my-screen-blank-out-for-the-duration-of-the-grub-boot-menu) ).  Once I got the grub menu back, I tried booting into Windows 7; it blue-screened during boot up.  System doesn't seem to like Microsoft's default VGA driver.  Since system crashes so soon after power up,  I can't even get it to boot into safe mode to load an Nvidia driver.  Win7 boots and works fine if I disable the GT240 via BIOS setup and use the on-board graphics controller.

I'm now convinced there's some hardware-interaction problem among motherboard, ram, and video card on the T110.  Guess I can try a different graphics card, but I'm not sure that'll fix the problem and not introduce new ones.  Strange thing is that Ubuntu 10.04 works flawlessly with the current setup.  Pretty lame motherboard if you ask me

If anyone have a solution, or suggestions, please post.  Thanks.

---

### Post by ptmoy on 2012-12-25
Ok, I have additional information that I believe proves problem is caused by the T110's hardware/BIOS.  Here's the scenarios I tried:


[LIST=1]
[*]16GB RAM with an Nvidia GT240 card
[LIST]
[*]Ubuntu 10.04 works fine
[*]Windows 7 wouldn't boot
[*]Ubuntu 12.04 wouldn't boot
[/LIST]
 
[*]16GB RAM with an Nvidia 9400GT card
[LIST]
[*]Ubuntu 10.04 works fine
[*]Windows 7 boots, but couldn't recognize the graphics card;
windows 7 generates a code-12 error.  see attached screen shot.
[IMG]http://ubuntuforums.org/data:image/jpeg;base64,[/IMG]
[*]Ubuntu 12.04 boots, but can't load Nvidia driver.

[IMG]http://ubuntuforums.org/F:\Capture.jpg[/IMG]
[/LIST]
 
[*]2GB RAM with an Nvidia 9400GT card
[LIST]
[*]Ubuntu 10.04, Windows 7, and Ubuntu 12.04 all work fine.
[/LIST]
 
[*]4GB RAM (2 x 2GB sticks) with an Nvidia 9400GT card
[LIST]
[*]same result as scenario 2.
[/LIST]
 
[*]4GB RAM (1 x 4GB stick) with an Nvdia 9400GT card
[LIST]
[*]same result as scenario 2.
[/LIST]
 
[/LIST]
  The windows 7 error message provides a big clue.  Question is what and how to disable; I've tried disabling various things via bios with no success.  I'm already on BIOS version 1.9.2, which is Dell's latest for the T110. Problem may also be power related.  According to Dell spec, the long pcie slot on the T110 is a pcie x8 full length slot, and not a pcie x16.  I read on the internet that some people have had good luck with Radeon 5450 cards on the T110; if I recall correctly, 5450s are low-power consuming, requiring only around 17-18 watts or so.   Only puzzle is how Ubuntu 10.04 is able to work around the problem.  Perhaps someone from the Ubuntu development team can weigh in.

Other suggestions welcome.

---

### Post by ptmoy on 2012-12-25
I think I figured it out.  It's a power-related issue.  The pcie x8 full length slot in the T110 is unable to supply the power required by my 9400GT and GT240 cards. I dug up a really old Geforce 7600 card and installed it, and got everything to work.  I speculate other cards will work as long as spec's below around 25watts or so.

---
